lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The monitor settles. The number holds. In a trauma bay still smelling of arrival — salt and rubber and the specific cold that clings to people who have been outside — the work is done.

lines.speaker: MAEVE
lines.text: BP's stable. Let's get a repeat lactate in twenty.

lines.speaker: DANA
lines.text: She's tolerating it. Good call on the second line.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: It is the longest sentence Dana has spoken to Maeve in two hours. It contains no history. This is, Theo understands, a kind of discipline that can only be learned from the inside of a wound that never fully closed.

lines.speaker: MAEVE
lines.text: Reza, can you take handover?

lines.speaker: REZA
lines.text: On it.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Dana strips her gloves. One. Two. The familiar small ceremony of the end of a thing. She drops them in the yellow bin by the door. Her bag is already over one shoulder. She is, Theo realises, already gone — the body just hasn't received the message yet.

lines.speaker: DANA
lines.text: I'll radio if we get another call.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Maeve's name goes unspoken. The absence of it lands in the bay like a stone dropped in still water. Maeve stands at the monitor, her back to the door, watching the number hold.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Theo has his tablet. He always has his tablet. On it, under a heading that will never become a formal complaint unless he crosses a specific line, is the third documented diversion in six weeks — tonight's, plus the chest pain who sat in the corridor on the fifth, plus the stroke who waited forty-one minutes for a bay on the twelfth. Three entries. Three nights on which the gap between Harte's efficiency figures and the care that actually arrived in this building told two entirely different stories.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Agnes had said the word — retaliation — and held the pause a beat longer than the conversation required. She had not glanced at the submission route field on his screen. She had not needed to.

lines.speaker: THEO
lines.text: Third one.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Maeve turns from the monitor. Dana is already past the curtain, already moving through the corridor beyond. Maeve stands exactly where she stood, her hands still. Theo watches her watch the space where Dana was.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The shift has hours left. The efficiency report will show two bays running below optimal throughput. It will not show what the bay currently holds: one woman at a monitor, watching a corridor, learning again the particular shape of a departure she has already survived once.

lines.speaker: MAEVE
lines.text: What's the overnight census?

lines.speaker: THEO
lines.text: Forty-two. Three pending triage.

lines.speaker: MAEVE
lines.text: Right.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She turns back to the board. Whatever was in her face for the three seconds she watched that corridor — whatever exact weight of Cork and February and every call they ran before the thing between them went wrong — is already gone. Contained. Administered to itself with the same clinical precision applied to everything else.

lines.speaker: THEO
lines.text: Maeve.

lines.speaker: MAEVE
lines.text: I know, Theo.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He does not finish the sentence. She has answered the one he almost asked, which differs from the one she would not answer if he asked it directly. The bay holds this: a monitor, a board, two people who have learned that some wounds announce their own presence, need nothing from either party, and will simply wait — for the next shift, and the one after — until something finally breaks open or finally seals.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Nothing is named. The shift has hours left to run. On Theo's tablet, the third entry sits with its cursor still blinking in the notes field — third documented diversion in six weeks — waiting for a name he has not yet found the courage to place after his own.
